2-1.v: Accounts receivable are reported at net realizable value — the amount the company actually expects to collect. Because some customers will inevitably not pay, the gross AR balance is reduced by an Allowance for Credit Losses (a contra-asset account with a credit balance).

2-1.l: Balance Sheet Presentation of Net AR
2-1.v:
Accounts receivable (gross)         $100,000
Less: Allowance for credit losses       (3,750)
Net accounts receivable               $ 96,250

The $3,750 allowance represents management's estimate of future uncollectible amounts. The $3,750 is also recorded as Credit Loss Expense on the income statement.
Inventory Cost Flow Assumptions — FIFO and LIFO

When inventory is purchased at different prices at different times, the company must choose which unit cost flows to COGS when items are sold. For the FMAA exam, only FIFO and LIFO are tested (LIFO is prohibited under IFRS).

2-1.l: Medina Company FIFO vs LIFO Example
2-1.v:
Beginning inventory:    1,000 units @ $5.00
Purchase March 3:       1,500 units @ $6.00
Purchase March 20:      1,000 units @ $7.00
Total available:        3,500 units; 2,400 sold; 1,100 in ending inventory
Total cost of available: (1,000×$5) + (1,500×$6) + (1,000×$7) = $5,000 + $9,000 + $7,000 = $21,000

FIFO:
  Ending inventory (newest 1,100 units): (1,000×$7) + (100×$6) = $7,000 + $600 = $7,600
  COGS: $21,000 − $7,600 = $13,400

LIFO:
  Ending inventory (oldest 1,100 units): (1,000×$5) + (100×$6) = $5,000 + $600 = $5,600
  COGS: $21,000 − $5,600 = $15,400

Difference: FIFO COGS is $2,000 LOWER than LIFO COGS in a period of rising prices.

Fixed assets (PP&E) are recorded at historical cost (all costs needed to get the asset ready for use). Depreciation allocates that cost over the asset's useful life — matching the cost to the periods that benefit. Land is NEVER depreciated.

r: (Cost − Salvage) ÷ Useful Life = same amount each year
Assets that generate equal benefit each year — easiest method
Book Value (beginning of year) × (2 ÷ Useful Life) = accelerated
Assets that provide more benefit in early years; stops when BV = salvage
Accelerated — similar effect to DDB but different calculation
(Cost − Salvage) ÷ Estimated Total Units × Actual Units Produced
Usage-based assets like machinery; depreciation varies with production

2-1.v:
Straight-Line annual depreciation: ($100,000 − $10,000) ÷ 4 = $22,500/year

Double Declining Balance (DDB rate = 2 ÷ 4 = 50%):
  Year 1: $100,000 × 50% = $50,000 | BV = $50,000
  Year 2: $50,000 × 50% = $25,000 | BV = $25,000
  Year 3: $25,000 × 50% = $12,500 | BV = $12,500
  Year 4: $12,500 − $10,000 salvage = $2,500 (stop at salvage value)
  Total depreciation: $50,000 + $25,000 + $12,500 + $2,500 = $90,000 = Depreciable base ✓

Balance sheet presentation:
  Fixed assets (cost)         $100,000
  Less: Accumulated depreciation (23,750)
  Book (carrying) value       $ 76,250

DDB key rule: NEVER depreciate below salvage value. In Year 4, DDB gives $6,250 but book value would drop to $6,250 which is below the $10,000 salvage — so only record $2,500 to reach exactly $10,000.

2-2.v: Revenue is recognized when (or as) a performance obligation is satisfied — that is, when the customer obtains control of the good or service. The 5-step model applies to all contracts with customers.

v: Step 1: Identify the contract with a customer (must be legally enforceable)
Step 2: Identify the separate performance obligations in the contract
Step 3: Determine the transaction price (consideration expected to be received)
Step 4: Allocate the transaction price to the separate performance obligations
Step 5: Recognize revenue when (or as) each performance obligation is satisfied
Cause and effect (matching): Expense recognized when the related revenue is recognized. COGS is the classic example — cost of inventory is expensed when the sale occurs.
Systematic and rational allocation: Expense allocated over periods benefited. Depreciation is the prime example — asset cost spread over useful life.
Immediate recognition: Expense recognized when incurred if no future benefit can be identified. Administrative costs, advertising, most period costs.

2-2.v: Gains and Losses on Disposal of Fixed Assets
When a fixed asset is sold, the gain or loss = Cash received − Book value (cost − accumulated depreciation). A gain means the asset sold for MORE than book value; a loss means it sold for LESS.

30 generators: Cost = $65,000 | Accumulated depreciation = $25,000 | Book value = $40,000
Sold for $28,000 cash (less $1,000 broker fee = $27,000 net proceeds)

Loss = $27,000 proceeds − $40,000 book value = $(13,000) loss

Journal entry:
  Dr  Cash                           27,000
  Dr  Accumulated Depreciation       25,000
  Dr  Loss on Sale of Equipment      13,000
      Cr  Equipment (cost)               65,000
Certain gains and losses bypass the income statement and are recorded directly in equity under Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (AOCI):

v: Unrealized gains/losses on available-for-sale debt securities
Effective portion of certain cash flow hedges

2-2.v: OCI items never appear in Net Income but DO appear in Comprehensive Income (Net Income + OCI). They accumulate in AOCI on the balance sheet until realized or reclassified.

2-3.v: When shares are issued, the par value goes to the Common Stock account; everything above par goes to Additional Paid-in Capital (APIC). Par value is always a very small stated amount and bears no relation to market price.

Example: Issue 10,000 shares at $15/share; par value = $1/share:
  Dr  Cash                        150,000
      Cr  Common Stock (10,000 × $1)    10,000
      Cr  APIC-Common Stock            140,000

No-par stock: Issue 10,000 shares at $15/share:
  Dr  Cash                        150,000
      Cr  Common Stock (full amount)   150,000

v: Declaration date: Dr Dividends (or Retained Earnings) / Cr Dividends Payable — liability created
Record date: No journal entry — determines which shareholders receive dividend
Payment date: Dr Dividends Payable / Cr Cash — liability settled

2-3.v:
Board declares $0.50/share dividend on 100,000 shares = $50,000 total.

Declaration date:
  Dr  Retained Earnings (or Dividends)    50,000
      Cr  Dividends Payable                   50,000

Payment date:
  Dr  Dividends Payable    50,000
      Cr  Cash                   50,000

Treasury stock is common stock that was issued and later repurchased by the company. It reduces equity (shown as a negative). It is recorded at cost; when reissued, any difference from cost goes to APIC, not to income.

Buy back 1,000 shares at $20/share:
  Dr  Treasury Stock    20,000
      Cr  Cash               20,000

Reissue 500 shares at $25/share:
  Dr  Cash (500 × $25)                  12,500
      Cr  Treasury Stock (500 × $20 cost)   10,000
      Cr  APIC-Treasury Stock                2,500
(Gain goes to APIC — never to income statement)

2-4.v: Every line item expressed as a percentage of a base amount. For the income statement, base = net sales revenue. For the balance sheet, base = total assets. This removes the effect of size differences, enabling comparison between companies or periods.

Net sales revenue:   $12,000,000   100.0%
Cost of goods sold:   (8,000,000)   66.7%
Gross profit:          4,000,000    33.3%
Operating expenses:   (2,500,000)   20.8%
Operating income:      1,500,000    12.5%
Net income:            1,218,857    10.2%

Insight: If COGS% rises from 66.7% to 70% next year, costs are rising faster than revenue — a warning signal.
Compare the same line item across multiple periods. Each amount is expressed as a percentage change from the base year. Reveals trends — is revenue growing? Are expenses growing faster than revenue?

Sales 20X1: $10,000,000 (base year = 100%)
Sales 20X2: $11,500,000 → 115% (15% growth)
Sales 20X3: $12,000,000 → 120% (20% growth over base)

COGS 20X1: $6,500,000 (65% of sales)
COGS 20X3: $8,000,000 (66.7% of sales)
→ COGS growing faster than sales: gross margin is shrinking.
Vertical = one year, compare items within that year. Horizontal = one item, compare across multiple years. Both together tell a more complete story.

2-5.v: Liquidity ratios measure a company's ability to pay short-term obligations. Higher ratios generally mean better short-term liquidity, though too high can indicate inefficient use of current assets.

r: Standard 2:1; lower may signal liquidity risk
(Cash + Marketable Securities + Net AR) ÷ Current Liabilities
Most conservative; only most liquid assets

2-5.v:
Current assets: Cash $50,000 | AR $80,000 | Inventory $120,000 | Prepaid $10,000
Current liabilities: AP $70,000 | Accrued expenses $30,000

Current ratio: ($50k+$80k+$120k+$10k) ÷ ($70k+$30k) = $260,000 ÷ $100,000 = 2.6
Quick ratio: ($50k+$80k) ÷ $100,000 = 1.3 (inventory excluded)
Cash ratio: $50,000 ÷ $100,000 = 0.5
Net working capital: $260,000 − $100,000 = $160,000

The quick ratio excludes inventory because inventory may be slow to convert to cash (especially in manufacturing). A current ratio above 2.0 and quick ratio above 1.0 are traditionally considered healthy, but always compare to industry norms.

2-6.v: Leverage ratios measure long-term solvency — the company's ability to meet long-term debt obligations. Higher leverage = more risk for creditors and equity holders (but also potential for higher returns through financial leverage).

r: How much of financing comes from debt vs equity; 2:1 = $2 debt per $1 equity
Proportion of assets financed by creditors; higher = more leveraged
How many times earnings cover interest; higher = safer for creditors

2-6.v:
Total assets: $500,000 | Total liabilities: $300,000 | Total equity: $200,000
Current liabilities: $80,000 | EBIT: $60,000 | Interest expense: $15,000

Debt to equity: $300,000 ÷ $200,000 = 1.5 (or 150%) — for every $1 of equity, $1.50 of debt
Long-term debt to equity: ($300,000 − $80,000) ÷ $200,000 = $220,000 ÷ $200,000 = 1.1
Debt to total assets: $300,000 ÷ $500,000 = 0.60 (60% creditor-financed)
Times interest earned: $60,000 ÷ $15,000 = 4.0× (earnings cover interest 4 times)

Financial leverage magnifies both gains and losses. A company with 60% debt financing earns a higher ROE when things go well — but faces higher risk of default when earnings decline. Creditors prefer lower debt ratios; shareholders sometimes prefer higher leverage for return enhancement.

2-7.v: Activity ratios measure how efficiently a company manages its assets — especially the working capital components: receivables, inventory, and payables. The days-based ratios directly connect to the operating and cash cycles.

r: Net Annual Credit Sales ÷ Average Gross AR
Number of times AR is collected per year; higher = faster collection
365 ÷ AR Turnover (or: Average AR ÷ Daily Credit Sales)
Average days to collect — compare to credit terms
How many times inventory sold and replaced per year
How many times AP is paid per year

2-7.v:
Net credit sales: $2,400,000 | Average AR: $200,000
COGS: $1,800,000 | Average inventory: $300,000

AR Turnover: $2,400,000 ÷ $200,000 = 12 times/year
Days' Sales in Receivables: 365 ÷ 12 = 30.4 days
(If credit terms are 30 days net, this is on-target)

Inventory Turnover: $1,800,000 ÷ $300,000 = 6 times/year
Days' Sales in Inventory: 365 ÷ 6 = 60.8 days
(Inventory sits on shelves an average of ~61 days before being sold)

Always compare activity ratios to: (1) the company's own credit/payment terms, (2) prior periods, and (3) industry benchmarks. A DSR of 45 days is excellent for a company with 60-day terms but concerning for one with 30-day terms.

2-8.v: Profitability ratios measure the company's ability to generate returns for its shareholders and cover its costs. Margins show profitability relative to sales; return ratios show profitability relative to assets or equity.

r: % of revenue remaining after direct production costs
% of revenue remaining after all operating costs (before interest & tax)
Final % of revenue kept as profit after all costs and taxes

2-8.v:
Net sales: $12,000,000
COGS: $8,000,000
Gross profit: $4,000,000 → Gross margin: 33.3%
Operating expenses: $2,500,000
Operating income: $1,500,000 → Operating margin: 12.5%
Interest & other: $25,000 income, $40,000 expense, $57,857 non-op gains
Income before tax: $1,542,857
Income tax (21%): $324,000
Net income: $1,218,857 → Net margin: 10.2%

Average total assets: $9,000,000
ROA: $1,218,857 ÷ $9,000,000 = 13.5%

Average equity: $4,500,000
ROE: $1,218,857 ÷ $4,500,000 = 27.1%

ROE > ROA when financial leverage is being used effectively — the company earns more on assets than it costs to finance them with debt. If ROE < ROA, leverage is destroying value (debt is too expensive relative to asset returns). This is called the leverage effect.
